does: This paper presents Disrupt-and-Rectify Smoothing (DR-Smoothing), a certified defense mechanism against jailbreaking attacks on large language models. The method implements a two-stage prompt processing pipeline that first disrupts malicious input prompts through noise injection, then rectifies them back to in-distribution text to prevent unpredictable model behavior. The authors provide theoretical analysis establishing tight bounds on defense success probability and required disruption strength for the generic smoothing framework. The approach simultaneously mitigates both token-level and prompt-level attacks while maintaining a balance between safety and model utility, offering formal guarantees on defense effectiveness beyond empirical robustness claims.
connects: This work extends denoised-smoothing adversarial defense techniques from computer vision to the language domain, adapting certified robustness approaches developed for image classifiers to protect LLM outputs. It builds on prior disrupt-only defense strategies that recognized the value of input perturbation for safety but suffered from generating out-of-distribution text that causes unpredictable LLM responses. The paper contributes to the growing body of work on certified defenses for neural networks, drawing on randomized smoothing theory and extending it with the insight that rectification enhances both robustness and model helpfulness. It responds to emerging concerns about systematic jailbreaking vulnerabilities in large language models and the limitations of purely empirical defense evaluations.
enables: This work opens pathways toward scalable, theoretically-grounded safety mechanisms for deployed language models that can defend against unknown attack variants. The certified robustness framework enables formal guarantees on defense margins, allowing practitioners to quantify safety tradeoffs and select appropriate disruption strengths. Future work can build on the two-stage processing paradigm to develop more sophisticated rectification methods that better preserve semantic intent while maintaining adversarial disruption. The theoretical framework provides a foundation for extending certified defenses to other language model vulnerabilities beyond jailbreaking. Additionally, the balance achieved between harmlessness and helpfulness suggests broader applications to other safety-utility tradeoffs in AI systems and potential extension to multimodal models.
